cards.front: What is a compound statement and where are they commonly used?
cards.back: A compound statement (block) is zero or more statements treated as a single statement, enclosed in braces { }. Commonly used with if statements to execute multiple statements conditionally.

cards.front: What are the two ways to access a namespace member?
cards.back:
• Using the scope resolution operator (namespace::member)
  • Using a using declaration or using directive (then access member directly)

cards.text: {{c1::Scope}} determines where an identifier can be accessed. {{c2::Duration}} determines when a variable is created and destroyed. {{c3::Linkage}} determines whether multiple declarations refer to the same object.

cards.front: What's the key difference between local and global variables?
cards.back: Local variables have block scope and automatic duration (created/destroyed with scope). Global variables have file scope and static duration (exist for program lifetime).

cards.front: How do you avoid variable shadowing?
cards.back: Use different names for variables in nested scopes, limit variable scope, enable compiler warnings (-Wshadow), and use 'g_' prefix for globals to distinguish them from locals.

cards.text: Use {{c1::internal linkage}} (static or unnamed namespace) for identifiers that should only be accessible within a single file. Use {{c2::external linkage}} for identifiers that need to be shared across files.

cards.front: What's the modern C++17 way to share constants across files?
cards.back: Use inline constexpr variables in a header file, preferably inside a namespace. This creates a single shared constant accessible from all files that include the header.

cards.front: What are the three different uses of the 'static' keyword in C++?
cards.back:
• Static local variables (persist between function calls)
  • Static global variables/functions (internal linkage)
  • Static duration (lifetime of program)

cards.front: What are three key reasons to avoid non-const global variables?
cards.back:
• Hard to debug (any function can modify them)
  • Create tight coupling (reduce modularity)
  • Initialization order issues across files

cards.explanation:
Master these core principles:
  • Keep variable scope as narrow as possible
  • Use const/constexpr for constants, never non-const globals
  • Understand scope, duration, and linkage for all identifiers
  • Use namespaces to organize code and prevent collisions
  • Prefer unnamed namespaces over 'static' for internal linkage
  • Use inline variables (C++17) for shared constants in headers
  • Avoid variable shadowing and 'using namespace' in headers

cards.text: The three types of linkage are {{c1::no linkage}} (local variables), {{c2::internal linkage}} (one file), and {{c3::external linkage}} (across files).
Keep variable scope as {{c1::narrow}} as possible and avoid {{c2::non-const global}} variables.
Use the {{c1::scope resolution operator}} ({{c2:::}}) to access namespace members: std{{c3::::}}cout
The keyword {{c1::static}} has three uses: static {{c2::local variables}}, static {{c3::global identifiers}} (internal linkage), and static {{c4::duration}}.
